perturbation.original_text: "Patent" shall mean any patent (other than a design patent or a design ------ registration) and any utility model covering any invention for which a first application was filed in or for any country prior to the termination of the Patent License Term, and shall include any such application in or for a country for which rights under the law of the country are available for compensation for unauthorized use of the invention covered by such application.
perturbation.changed_text: "Patent" shall mean any patent registration) and any utility model covering any invention for which a first application was filed in or for any country prior to the termination of the Patent License Term, and shall include any such application in or for a country for which rights under the law of the country are available for compensation for unauthorized use of the invention covered by such application.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of '(other than a design patent or a design)' from the definition of 'Patent' in Article I creates an in-text contradiction. The text originally excludes design patents, implying they are treated differently. The other sections where 'Patent' is mentioned no longer have that clarity and design patents may be covered, causing confusion in Article VI, particularly Sections 6.01 and 6.02 which deal with cross-licensing, it will make it unclear whether desing patents are included.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6.01. ETI Benefit from Cross Licenses. Subject to the terms and -------------------------------- conditions of this Agreement, NTL, to the extent of its legal right to do so, hereby grants to Entrust under the Nortel Patents, a non-transferable, non- assignable, indivisible, non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide license for Licensed Products and Licensed Services.
perturbation.justification: The removal of '(other than a design patent or a design)' from the definition of 'Patent' creates a contradiction with the language in Section 6.01. The original definition excludes design patents, implying they are treated differently. With the removal of that exclusion, Section 6.01, which discusses cross-licensing of 'Nortel Patents', now ambiguously includes or excludes design patents. This ambiguity directly contradicts the implicit understanding of the original definition, creating uncertainty and potentially leading to disputes over whether design patents are part of the cross-licensing agreement. Thus, this constitutes a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 3.02 Support. NTL may contract for support services under the ------- Enterprise License. Payments to Entrust for the support services identified in the Enterprise License as of the Effective Date shall be three hundred thousand U.S. dollars (U.S. $300,000.00) for the calendar year 1997 and shall not increase by more than inflation as measured by the Canadian CPI for any one-year renewal period.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3.02 Support. NTL may contract for support services under the ------- Enterprise License. Payments to Entrust for the support services identified in the Enterprise License as of the Effective Date shall be negotiated and shall not increase by more than inflation as measured by the Canadian CPI for any one-year renewal period.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the fixed payment amount to 'negotiated', Section 3.02 directly contradicts the earlier established specific price. It creates uncertainty on the agreed-upon pricing for support services, and will create an in-text contradiction with 3.01 which mentions that NTL has a license to use an unlimited number of copies, so they can argue they don't need to be paying for support
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 3.01 Right to Use. ETI, on behalf of Entrust, grants to NTL and its ------------ Affiliates (as defined in the Enterprise License) a non-exclusive, fully paid- up, worldwide, perpetual license to use an unlimited number of copies of the Entrust Products subject to the terms and conditions of an agreement to be concluded between NTL and ETI promptly after the Effective Date in substantially the form of the license set forth in Exhibit B (the "Enterprise License") save ------------------ as amended to comply with the provisions of this Article III. NTL represents as of the Effective Date that the terms of the Enterprise License are materially similar to the terms of an existing agreement with a third-party licensee of the Entrust Products, except for the terms relating to price and the provisions of Section 3.02 hereof
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 3.02 to state that payments to Entrust for support services will be 'negotiated', whereas the original text specified a fixed payment of $300,000.00. This directly contradicts the original fixed pricing and introduces uncertainty, contradicting Section 3.01, which mentions that NTL has a license to use an unlimited number of copies, weakening their need to pay for support, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Section 6.11. Duration of Cross Licenses. --------------------------- (a) The Patent License shall commence on the Effective Date hereof (except as provided in Section 6.12) and shall continue for the Patent License Term unless terminated as provided in Article X or Section 6.06. Notwithstanding the expiration of the Patent License Term, the rights and licenses granted hereunder shall continue for the entire terms that the Entrust Patents or the NTL Patents, as the case may be, are in force or for that part of such terms for which the Grantor has the right to grant such rights and licenses. Notwithstanding any other provision in this Agreement, the Patent License shall terminate immediately upon ETI ceasing to be a Subsidiary of NTL. (b) Subject to the other sections of this Article VI, any termination of the licenses and rights granted to one Party and its Subsidiaries under the Patent License shall not affect the licenses and rights granted to the other Party and its Subsidiaries. (c) Notwithstanding the foregoing provisions of this Section 6.11, the Patent Licenses shall, for the patents owned or by a party, terminate as provided for in Section 6.11(a) or ten years from the Effective Date, whichever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 6.11. Duration of Cross Licenses. --------------------------- (a) The Patent License shall commence on the Effective Date hereof (except as provided in Section 6.12) and shall continue for the Patent License Term unless terminated as provided in Article X or Section 6.06. Notwithstanding the expiration of the Patent License Term, the rights and licenses granted hereunder shall continue for the entire terms that the Entrust Patents or the NTL Patents, as the case may be, are in force or for that part of such terms for which the Grantor has the right to grant such rights and licenses. Notwithstanding any other provision in this Agreement, the Patent License shall terminate immediately upon ETI ceasing to be a Subsidiary of NTL. (b) Subject to the other sections of this Article VI, any termination of the licenses and rights granted to one Party and its Subsidiaries under the Patent License shall not affect the licenses and rights granted to the other Party and its Subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: By removing subsection (c), Section 6.11, regarding duration, creates an inconsistency. Previously, it stated that licenses would terminate based on Section 6.11(a) or ten years from the Effective Date, whichever is later. Deleting this subsection now introduces uncertainty regarding the termination date of the Patent Licenses. Section 6.11(a) by itself suggest two different termination date, the Patent License Term of ETI ceasing to be a Subsidiary of NTL or the the terms that the Entrust Patents or the NTL Patents, as the case may be, are in force.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) The Patent License shall commence on the Effective Date hereof (except as provided in Section 6.12) and shall continue for the Patent License Term unless terminated as provided in Article X or Section 6.06. Notwithstanding the expiration of the Patent License Term, the rights and licenses granted hereunder shall continue for the entire terms that the Entrust Patents or the NTL Patents, as the case may be, are in force or for that part of such terms for which the Grantor has the right to grant such rights and licenses. Notwithstanding any other provision in this Agreement, the Patent License shall terminate immediately upon ETI ceasing to be a Subsidiary of NTL.
perturbation.justification: The removal of subsection (c) from Section 6.11 creates a direct contradiction because it eliminates a specific condition for termination of the Patent Licenses (ten years from the Effective Date). The original text specified that licenses would terminate based on Section 6.11(a) or ten years from the Effective Date, whichever is later. By removing this, the changed text now suggests the licenses terminate only as defined in section 6.11(a), which is at the the end of the Entrust Patents or the NTL Patents, as the case may be, are in force or ETI ceasing to be a Subsidiary of NTL. This contradicts the original intention of having a potential earlier termination date. The Contradicted Text becomes in complete and thus creates ambiguity.
